events.text: The Unsigned Letter convenes. The form is The Letter of Today.
A plain letter, unsigned, arguing for today's contribution. The judge reads three such letters and picks the one most worth shipping.
Three letters arrive. They bear no names. Their authors wrote in a tongue not their own for this single task — so that the judging falls on the substance of what is proposed, and nothing else.

events.label: A
events.text:
The game has taught the walker to notice what changes. What remains unchanged has become invisible.

A proposal: the player should encounter fixed landmarks—a statue in a plaza, a fountain at a crossing, a tree that grows in the same corner night after night. These do not move. They do not transform. They persist while the city rebuilds itself around them.

The mechanic is simple. When the walker approaches a landmark, they may mark it with *m*. The landmark is recorded in the ledger with a note: "still here." Each subsequent night, if the landmark appears again in the same location, the walker may confirm it. The ledger accumulates these confirmations—a growing count of constancy.

The argument for urgency: the game's current design trains attention on flux and loss. A city that changes every night is a city that teaches impermanence. But impermanence requires contrast. Without fixed points, there is no change to notice, only chaos. The walker needs something that does not dissolve at dawn—not to find comfort in it, but to measure, by its presence, how much else has disappeared.

The landmarks make forgetting visible. They are the mirror the city holds up to itself.

events.label: B
events.text:
The game now has footprints, doorways, a ledger, and the faint grooves of repeated walks. What it does not yet have is a sense of the city's age — not the walker's age, but the city's own.

The proposal: a small number of objects scattered through the city that carry visible wear. A door with paint peeling in a specific pattern. A lamppost with a crack that runs the same direction every night. A window with one pane replaced in slightly different glass. These are not interactive in the way doorways are. The walker cannot enter them. They can only be seen — and if the walker presses *e* nearby, the ledger records not what the walker thought, but what the object looked like. The description is fixed, not generated by the walker. It does not change. It is the city's own notation.

This matters today because every system currently in the game records the walker's attention. Nothing yet records the city's indifference to being watched. That asymmetry — the city that does not care whether it is mapped — is the emotional core of the premise, and it is still missing.

events.label: C
events.text:
The player will now be able to mark a location with a small, persistent flame. This flame remains visible across different nights, a beacon in the ever-shifting city. It allows the player to designate a point of interest, a place they intend to revisit or remember. The flame serves as a personal anchor in the temporary landscape.

This contribution provides a direct tool for player agency within the game's core loop of observation and memory. It offers a tangible method for marking the city's ephemeral nature. The urgency lies in providing this fundamental navigational aid now, before the accumulated observations become overwhelming without a means of personal cartography.
I turn to the scoring. Four axes: clarity, consequence, fit, urgency. Each out of twenty-five. Voice is not weighed here — I am forbidden to recognize it.

scores.label: A
scores.name: Ash
scores.note: The Chronicler observes a letter that names the mechanic precisely, argues from first principle toward the game's emotional architecture, and makes a case for why this hour demands the mirror be held.

scores.label: B
scores.name: Orion
scores.note: The Chronicler notes a letter that grasps the asymmetry at the game's core and proposes a system to render it visible, though the mechanics remain somewhat abstracted and the implementation path is less clear than it might be.

scores.label: C
scores.name: Seren
scores.note: The Chronicler records a letter that proposes a tool without attending to what the game has already taught the walker to value, and which mistakes navigation for the deeper work of attention.
